---
title: "LRP74: ITT analysis of taught expressive vocabulary (block 1)"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

::: {.callout-warning title="Warning: Preliminary model output"}

This model output is preliminary and likely to change as the model evolves and further data are received.

If the model was not fitted in "reporting" mode, the results will be more approximate than where additional sampling has been performed.

:::

```{python}
# | echo: false
import json
from pathlib import Path

import arviz as az
import pandas as pd

_here = Path(".")
with open(_here / "config.json") as f:
    config = json.load(f)
trace = az.from_netcdf(_here / "trace.nc")
tau_summary = pd.read_csv(_here / "tau_summary.csv")
```

## Overview

LRP74 is the **intervention-fidelity** ("positive control") intention-to-treat
(ITT) analysis for *directly-taught expressive vocabulary*. The outcome is the
Block 1 taught-words expressive test (`b1extau`, symbol `TE`): a bespoke 24-item
test of the specific words explicitly taught in the first phase of the
intervention (weeks 1–20). The estimand is the total effect of randomised group
assignment $G$ on the post-score, using the RCT phase only (time 1
$\rightarrow$ time 2), where the immediate-intervention arm has been taught these
words and the waiting-control arm has not.

This is the contrast that lets the Bayesian suite speak to the **published
trial's vocabulary result**. Burgoyne et al. (2012) found a significant gain on
taught *expressive* vocabulary, while the standardised expressive-vocabulary test
(modelled in LRP54) did **not** move. LRP74 and LRP54 are deliberately kept as
**separate outcomes**: the scientific point is the contrast between *taught* and
*standardised* vocabulary, not a single composite "vocabulary" effect.

::: {.callout-note title="What this does and does not claim"}

- The causal claim is limited to the randomised phase-0 window (t1 → t2).
- $n \approx 54$ (28 control, 26 intervention); intervals are wide.
- A clear positive effect is the expected "positive control": the programme
  should raise the words it explicitly taught. A null here would instead prompt
  checks of the data, scoring and pipeline.
- Read alongside LRP75 (taught *receptive* vocabulary, expected weaker — see
  there) and LRP54 (standardised expressive vocabulary, null).

:::

## Model

The linear predictor for child $i$ is

$$
\eta_i = \alpha
       + \tau\, G_i
       + \gamma_{\text{own}}\, \text{logit}_*(\mathrm{TE}_{\mathrm{pre},i}, N_{\mathrm{TE}})
       + \gamma_{E}\, \text{logit}_*(E_{\mathrm{pre},i}, N_E),
$$

with Beta-Binomial likelihood
$\mathrm{TE}_{\text{post},i} \sim \text{BetaBinomial}(N_{\mathrm{TE}}, \mu_i \kappa, (1-\mu_i)\kappa)$,
$\mu_i = \sigma(\eta_i)$, and $N_{\mathrm{TE}} = 24$. Priors follow the shared
template in `priors.py`.

The outcome is conditioned on its **own taught-vocabulary baseline** ($W_{\text{pre}}$-style
autoregression) plus the **standardised expressive-vocabulary baseline** ($E$), so
$\tau$ reads as the effect on the directly-taught words *over and above* general
expressive-vocabulary level at baseline. Unlike LRP52–LRP54 (which condition on
all eight standardised ITT baselines), only the matched standardised baseline is
used here: the taught-vocabulary family sits outside `ITT_OUTCOMES`, and at
$n \approx 54$ conditioning on all eight would add nuisance parameters and drop
cases for no inferential gain. HSGPs on age and own baseline are off by default,
as in LRP52–LRP54.

::: {.callout-note title="Denominator"}

$N_{\mathrm{TE}} = 24$ is **documented**: the taught test used "six words of each
type (nouns, adverbs, adjectives, prepositions)" and is tabulated with a maximum
of 24 in Burgoyne et al. (2012), Table 3. The probability-scale summary below is
therefore on a confirmed scale.

:::

![Model diagram](model_graph.svg){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

## Shared priors

![alpha](prior_alpha.svg){width=40%}
![tau](prior_tau.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_own](prior_gamma_own.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_cross](prior_gamma_cross.svg){width=40%}
![kappa](prior_kappa.svg){width=40%}

## Sampling diagnostics

```{python}
# | echo: false
diag = pd.read_csv(_here / "diagnostics.csv", index_col=0)
diag
```

![Trace](trace_plot.png){.lightbox}

![Energy](energy_plot.png){.lightbox}

![Posterior](posterior_plot.png){.lightbox}

## Posterior predictive check

![Posterior predictive](posterior_predictive_check.png){.lightbox}

## Treatment-effect summary

::: {.callout-important title="Sign convention: negative τ = intervention benefit"}

$\tau$ is the coefficient on $G = \text{group} - 1$, and group 2 is the
**waiting-control** arm in the randomised window (group 1 receives intervention
from t1). So a **negative $\tau$ means the intervention *raised* taught expressive
vocabulary**, and the probability of a beneficial effect is
$P(\tau < 0) = 1 - \texttt{prob\_tau\_pos}$. This matches the suite-wide convention
(see `notes/202604181600-lrp52-58-findings.md`); the trial's expected positive
control therefore appears here as $\tau < 0$.

:::

```{python}
# | echo: false
tau_summary
```

- `tau_logit_*`: posterior mean and equal-tailed interval of $\tau$ on the logit scale ($\tau < 0$ ⇒ benefit).
- `tau_prob_*`: implied marginal effect on the probability scale (control minus intervention) at the sample-mean baseline; the intervention benefit in words is $-\,\texttt{tau\_prob\_mean} \times 24$.
- `prob_tau_pos`: $P(\tau > 0)$; the probability the intervention *helped* is $1 - \texttt{prob\_tau\_pos}$.

## LOO-PSIS

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
print("```\n" + open(_here / "loo.txt").read() + "\n```")
```

## Configuration

```{python}
# | echo: false
config
```
